Where the full use of country systems is not possible, the provider of development co-operation will state the reasons for non-use […]” (p. 5)
z.positioning: This excerpt demonstrates that ultimate authority over the use of recipient-country systems remains with development cooperation providers, who retain the ability to determine whether local institutions are sufficiently reliable or effective. Although the language of ownership and alignment is present, decision-making power remains concentrated within donor organizations and international development institutions. The framework therefore reflects a donor-led architecture in which Northern actors continue to define the conditions under which recipient-country systems are recognized and utilized. As such, this source ranks at a -5 on the Western side of the axis.

author: Easterly
shortTitle: The White Man's Burden

x.citation: “If you want to work on issues at a higher level, there should also be Searchers for how to make piecemeal changes to the foreign aid system to give more power and money to the working-level Searchers. One piecemeal change is honest and independent evaluation of aid agencies, which would make possible rewards for finding things that work and redirection of money to do more of those things. Searchers could think of mechanisms to let the poor themselves show what they want most and what they don’t. We will see that there is much scope for improvement just by having the West follow the rule ‘First do no harm.’ This book will offer plenty more suggestions for experimental improvements to Western assistance, but don’t expect a Big Plan to reform foreign aid. The only Big Plan is to discontinue the Big Plans. The only Big Answer is that there is no Big Answer.” (p. 31)
x.positioning: This excerpt rejects large-scale, centrally planned approaches to development and instead advocates decentralized, experimental, and locally responsive forms of aid. Easterly criticizes traditional planning-based development models and argues for empowering 'Searchers' who can identify context-specific solutions. While this represents a significant departure from conventional aid practices, the author does not call for the abolition of development assistance or the broader global economic system. Rather, he advocates a substantial reorientation of aid toward adaptive and market-oriented approaches. Consequently, this source occupies a moderately transformative position, ranking at a 4 on the transformation side of the axis.

y.citation: “We will see in the rest of the book the refreshing changes that can happen once the patronizing mind-set is abandoned—from ending conditions placed on aid and IMF loans, to ending military interventions, to giving matching grants that increase the opportunities of individuals.” (p. 23)
y.positioning: This excerpt emphasizes empowering individuals rather than governments or large institutions. Easterly argues against paternalistic aid practices and instead supports mechanisms that expand opportunities for individuals through market-based approaches and decentralized decision-making. The emphasis on personal agency, individual incentives, and localized problem-solving places responsibility for development largely at the individual level rather than within collective institutions or state structures. As such, this source ranks at an 8 on the individual side of the axis.

z.citation: “Poor people have already accomplished far more for themselves than the Planners have accomplished for them, as we will see in a chapter on ‘home-grown development.’ Although the West could help alleviate more of the poor’s sufferings if it relied more on Searchers in aid agencies and those on the ground such as Etenesh Ayele, the West cannot transform the Rest. It is a fantasy to think that the West can change complex societies with very different histories and cultures into some image of itself. The main hope for the poor is for them to be their own Searchers, borrowing ideas and technology from the West when it suits them to do so.” (pp. 24)
z.positioning: This excerpt strongly criticizes Western paternalism and the assumption that external actors can engineer development in other societies. Easterly argues that local populations possess the capacity to identify and pursue their own development paths and that Western actors should abandon efforts to impose their own models. However, the proposed alternatives remain rooted in liberal market principles and individual entrepreneurship, reflecting a Western intellectual framework even while criticizing Western interventionism. Consequently, the passage partially decentralizes Western authority while retaining market-liberal assumptions, resulting in a moderately Western orientation that ranks at a -4 on the Western side of the axis.

author: Hickel
shortTitle: Apartheid in the World Bank and the IMF

x.citation: “There have long been calls by civil society and political leaders in the global South to democratise the World Bank and the IMF. At minimum, critics have argued that the leaders of these institutions should be elected in a transparent process. And they have called for a ‘double majority’ system such that significant decisions should require not only shareholder majorities but also member-state majorities. This would ensure that global South countries have a fairer say in the decisions that affect them, and power to block harmful policies [...] Nelson Mandela called for democratic reform of voting power at the World Bank and the IMF. This represents an historic opening, and campaigners should seize it. If we want to have a shot at a fairer global economy, we need to start by decolonising the institutions of economic governance.” (Hickel, 2020)
x.positioning: This excerpt advocates for democratizing existing international financial institutions by reforming their governance structures and voting arrangements. Hickel argues that institutions such as the World Bank and IMF should become more representative and accountable to Global South countries, but he does not call for their abolition or replacement. Instead, the proposed changes seek to decolonize and redistribute power within existing institutions. Although these reforms would significantly alter global economic governance, they remain largely institutional rather than revolutionary in nature. Consequently, this source occupies a moderately transformative position, ranking at a 3 on the transformation side of the axis.

y.citation: “Critics have called for a ‘double majority’ system such that significant decisions should require not only shareholder majorities but also member-state majorities […] This would ensure that global South countries have a fairer say in the decisions that affect them, and power to block harmful policies.” (Hickel, 2020)
y.positioning: This excerpt emphasizes collective decision-making and institutional representation by advocating reforms that would increase the political voice of member states, particularly those in the Global South. The proposed double-majority system prioritizes shared governance, coalition-building, and multilateral decision-making rather than individual leadership or technocratic expertise. The emphasis on institutional representation and collective political agency places this perspective strongly on the collective side of the axis, ranking at a 6 on the collective side.

z.citation: “For every vote that the average person in the global North has, the average person in the global South has only one-eighth of a vote (and the average South Asian has only one-twentieth of a vote).” (Hickel, 2020)
z.positioning: This excerpt directly highlights the unequal distribution of decision-making power within global economic institutions and demonstrates how countries and populations in the Global South remain systematically underrepresented. Hickel explicitly argues for redistributing authority toward Global South countries and increasing their ability to influence or block decisions that affect them. By centering Global South representation and challenging Northern dominance within international institutions, the passage strongly aligns with the Global South side of the axis, ranking at an 8 on the Global South side.

author: Hickel
shortTitle: Degrowth Is About Global Justice

x.citation: “Degrowth is a call to liberate the South from imperial appropriation and decolonise the atmosphere [...] It is overwhelmingly the rich countries of the Global North: the United States, Canada, Europe, Israel, Australia, New Zealand and Japan [...] collectively responsible for 92 per cent of excess emissions. They have colonised the atmospheric commons for their own enrichment.”
x.positioning: This excerpt locates the ecological crisis within the historical structures of imperialism, capitalist accumulation, and unequal resource consumption by the Global North. Rather than advocating reforms to existing development or environmental governance systems, the degrowth perspective challenges the underlying logic of perpetual economic growth and calls for a fundamental restructuring of the global economy. By framing environmental degradation as a consequence of imperial appropriation and colonial relations, the author advances a transformative critique that extends beyond contemporary development institutions. Consequently, this source ranks at a 7 on the transformation side of the axis.

y.citation: “The transition we need will not come easily. It requires grassroots movements capable of challenging the powerful class factions that benefit so prodigiously from the status quo. To succeed, the environmentalist movement will need to build strong alliances with labour unions and other working-class formations... to enable anyone to train for and participate in socially important and ecologically regenerative work [...]” (p. 18)
y.positioning: This excerpt emphasizes collective political action, social movements, and alliances among labor organizations, environmental groups, and working-class communities. The proposed transition depends upon broad-based mobilization and collective struggle rather than individual behavior change or technocratic intervention. By highlighting grassroots organizing and coalition-building as essential mechanisms for transformation, the passage strongly aligns with the collective side of the axis, ranking at a 7 on the collective side.

z.citation: “Virtually every major declaration that has come from Global South movements has the same message: the world economy is imperialist in nature and the ecological crisis is its consequence. Green politics in the Global North need to grapple with this analysis and align with the demands of Southern movements.”
z.positioning: This excerpt explicitly centers the analyses and demands of Global South movements, presenting them as essential sources of knowledge and political leadership regarding the ecological crisis. The author argues that actors in the Global North should respond to and align themselves with Southern perspectives rather than imposing their own solutions. Although responsibility for change is placed largely upon the Global North because of its historical contribution to environmental degradation, the passage nevertheless recognizes Global South movements as possessing important intellectual and political agency. Consequently, this source aligns strongly with the Global South side of the axis, ranking at a 6 on the Global South side.

author: Bridgetown Initiative
shortTitle: Bridgetown Initiative

x.citation: “Tinkering at the margins of a broken system is akin to rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic. It is time to act in solidarity for people and planet.” (p. 2)
x.positioning: This excerpt explicitly rejects incremental reform and argues that minor adjustments to existing systems are insufficient to address contemporary social, economic, and environmental crises. By describing the current system as fundamentally broken, the authors suggest that substantial structural change is necessary rather than merely improving existing development and financial arrangements. Although the passage does not specify the exact institutional alternatives required, its rejection of piecemeal reform and emphasis on solidarity indicate a strongly transformative orientation. Consequently, this source ranks at an 8 on the transformation side of the axis.

y.citation: “The commitment of developing countries to establish policy frameworks that preserve debt sustainability, while defining and delivering on robust plans to promote socially-inclusive, equitable, climate-resilient and environmentally sustainable development, must be supported. Such support must be realized through official development assistance and development finance at a sufficient scale to enable country-owned, structural transformation strategies and move them up the value chain.” (p. 3)
y.positioning: This excerpt emphasizes cooperation between developing countries and international development partners in achieving sustainable and inclusive development goals. While it stresses country ownership and nationally driven development strategies, it also argues that these efforts require external support through official development assistance and development finance. The passage therefore combines elements of national agency with collaborative international support, placing it moderately on the collective side of the axis. Consequently, this source ranks at a 4 on the collective side.

z.citation: “We call on international financial institutions to give a stronger voice to developing countries in their governance and decision-making [...] and to include climate vulnerability, natural capital and biodiversity conservation needs in their criteria for allocating concessional finance, addressing the inequity of many countries being classified as ineligible solely on a GNI per capita basis.” (p. 4)
z.positioning: This excerpt explicitly advocates for greater representation and decision-making power for developing countries within international financial institutions. It critiques existing allocation criteria and governance arrangements that disadvantage climate-vulnerable and lower-income countries, arguing for reforms that better reflect the experiences and needs of the Global South. Although responsibility for implementing these changes remains largely with international institutions and wealthier countries, the passage strongly emphasizes the need to expand the influence and agency of developing nations. As such, this source ranks at a 3 on the Global South side of the axis.

author: Mottley
shortTitle: COP26 World Leaders Summit - Opening Ceremony

x.citation: “On adaptation—Adaptation finance remains only 25% – NOT the 50:50 split needed given the warming that is already taking place. Climate finance to frontline SIDS declined by 25% in 2019. Failure to provide this critical finance and that of loss and damage is measured in lives and livelihoods being lost in our communities. It is immoral and unjust. If Glasgow is to deliver on the promises of Paris, it must close these three gaps.” (p. 1)
x.positioning: This excerpt criticizes the insufficient allocation of adaptation finance and the failure of wealthy countries to fulfill their existing climate commitments. Mottley argues that international agreements such as the Paris Agreement should be implemented more equitably and that promised financial support for adaptation and loss and damage must be delivered. The proposed solution operates within existing climate governance institutions and international agreements rather than calling for their replacement. Consequently, this source reflects a reform-oriented approach that seeks to improve the effectiveness and fairness of current systems, ranking at a -6 on the reform side of the axis.

y.citation: “What excuse should we give for their failure? IN THE WORDS OF EDDY GRANT ‘WILL THEY MOURN US ON THE FRONTLINE?’ When will we as world leaders address the pressing issues that cause our people to worry, be it climate or vaccines? When will leaders lead? Our people are watching and taking note.” (p. 2)
y.positioning: This excerpt emphasizes the responsibility of political leaders to respond to pressing global challenges such as climate change and public health crises. Mottley appeals directly to world leaders to demonstrate political leadership and accountability, suggesting that effective action depends upon leadership at the highest political levels. Although the speech speaks on behalf of vulnerable populations and frontline communities, the proposed agency rests primarily with political leaders and governments rather than collective social movements or grassroots actors. As such, this source ranks at a 5 on the individual side of the axis.

z.citation: “So I ask—what must we say to our people living on the frontline in the Caribbean, Africa and the Pacific when both ambition and some of the needed faces are absent?” (p. 1)
z.positioning: This excerpt centers the experiences of climate-vulnerable regions in the Caribbean, Africa, and the Pacific, emphasizing the disproportionate impacts these communities face despite contributing relatively little to global emissions. Mottley highlights the inequities embedded within existing climate governance arrangements and calls attention to the responsibilities of wealthier countries to fulfill their commitments. While the speech seeks greater recognition and inclusion of Global South concerns, it also relies on action by the Global North and existing international institutions to achieve these goals. Consequently, this source strongly aligns with the Global South side of the axis, ranking at a 7 on the Global South side.

author: Sanakra
shortTitle: A United Front Against Debt - Speech Before the Organization of African Union.

x.citation: “Debt is neo-colonialism, in which colonizers have transformed themselves into ‘technical assistants.’ We should rather say ‘technical assassins.’ They present us with financing, with financial backers. As if someone’s backing could create development. We have been advised to go to these lenders. We have been offered nice financial arrangements. We have been indebted for 50, 60 years and even longer. That means we have been forced to compromise our people for over 50 years [...] Debt cannot be repaid, first because if we don’t repay, lenders will not die. That is for sure. But if we repay, we are going to die.”
x.positioning: This excerpt presents external debt as a continuation of colonial domination and argues that international lending mechanisms reproduce relationships of dependency and exploitation. Sankara rejects the legitimacy of debt obligations altogether, describing technical assistance and financial lending as instruments of neo-colonial control. Rather than advocating reforms to existing financial institutions or debt management practices, he fundamentally challenges the moral and political foundations of the international debt system. Consequently, this source occupies a strongly transformative position, ranking at a 7 on the transformation side of the axis.

y.citation: “Debt cannot be repaid, first because if we don't repay, lenders will not die. But if we repay, we are going to die. We think that debt has to be seen from the standpoint of its origins. Debt's origins come from colonialism's origins. Those who lent us money are those who colonized us. We had no connections with this debt. Therefore we cannot pay for it [...] If Burkina Faso stands alone in refusing to pay, I will not be here for the next conference!”
y.positioning: This excerpt emphasizes Sankara's personal willingness to confront international creditors and challenge the legitimacy of debt repayment despite the political risks involved. The passage presents leadership and political courage as essential components of resistance, with Sankara explicitly acknowledging the personal consequences of acting alone. While the broader argument concerns collective experiences of colonialism and debt, the emphasis within this passage falls upon decisive political leadership and individual action. Consequently, this source ranks at a 5 on the individual side of the axis.

z.citation: “We cannot repay but the others owe us what the greatest wealth could never repay, that is blood debt. Our blood had flowed. We hear about the Marshall Plan that rebuilt Europe’s economy. But we never hear about the African plan which allowed Europe to face Hitlerian hordes when their economies and their stability were at stake. Who saved Europe? Africa. It is rarely mentioned, to such a point that we cannot be the accomplices of that thankless silence.”
z.positioning: This excerpt strongly centers African historical experiences, contributions, and grievances, arguing that Europe owes a moral and historical debt to Africa rather than the reverse. Sankara challenges dominant historical narratives that privilege European recovery and development while ignoring African sacrifices and contributions. By emphasizing African agency, historical memory, and the legitimacy of African demands for justice and recognition, the passage directly contests Eurocentric understandings of development and international obligation. Consequently, this source aligns very strongly with the Global South side of the axis, ranking at a 9 on the Global South side.
